text: The surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully perform sharp dissection, attempting to separate the duodenum from the gallbladder. The suction irrigator is employed to clear the field, revealing a stone inside the gallbladder.

The surgeon enlarges the incision using hook electrocautery and extracts a large stone from the gallbladder, providing a view inside to help orient the surgical field relative to the common bile duct.

The surgeon attempts to remove more of the duodenum from the gallbladder while excising as much of the gallbladder wall as possible. The suction irrigator is used to assist in this process. The surgeon tries to orient themselves to locate the common bile duct but decides to abort this part of the procedure due to extensive adhesions.

The surgeon switches from white mode to near-infrared mode, revealing bile leaking from the cystic duct. Blunt dissection is used to remove more of the gallbladder wall from the cystic plate of the liver, although the ICG imaging does not provide useful guidance.
The surgeon encounters bleeding from the liver and applies a hemostatic agent, resembling snow, to the area while applying pressure to control the bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the area of bleeding from the liver bed and confirms hemostasis. The cystic duct opening inside the gallbladder is closed using two 5-0 PDS sutures, carefully avoiding deep suturing to prevent stricture of the common bile duct.

The camera switches from white mode to near infrared mode, revealing no further bile leak from the cystic duct. Due to extensive adhesions, an intraoperative EGD and leak test are performed by submerging the duodenum in saline, showing bubbling that indicates an enterotomy. A small injury to the duodenum is visible and is repaired using an absorbable barb suture in a running fashion.
